mnemonics.title: Entropy = Expected Surprise
mnemonics.content: Entropy measures the average 'surprise' or uncertainty in a random variable. Higher entropy = more surprise = more unpredictable. Formula: H(X) = -Σ p(x)log₂p(x). Remember: Flat distribution (equal probabilities) = MAXIMUM entropy.

mnemonics.title: Mutual Information = Information Shared
mnemonics.content: Mutual Information I(X;Y) measures how much knowing X reduces uncertainty about Y (and vice versa). Think of it as the 'overlap' of information between two variables. I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y).

mnemonics.title: KL Divergence = Distribution Distance
mnemonics.content: KL Divergence D_KL(P||Q) measures 'how different' distribution P is from distribution Q. It's NOT symmetric (unlike distance). Always ≥ 0 (Gibbs inequality). Used in ML for measuring loss when approximating distributions.

mnemonics.title: Chain Rule for Entropy
mnemonics.content: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X). Total uncertainty = uncertainty in X + remaining uncertainty in Y after knowing X. Useful for understanding joint vs conditional entropy.

mnemonics.title: Entropy Units: Bits and Nats
mnemonics.content: Log base 2 = bits (used in CS/IT), Natural log = nats (used in physics/math). When calculating, match log base to desired unit. Remember: log₂(2) = 1 bit.

acronyms.usage: Use when measuring uncertainty/disorder in a single random variable. Higher H(X) = more unpredictable.

acronyms.expansion: Conditional Entropy of Y given X
acronyms.usage: Use when you want to know uncertainty in Y after knowing X. If H(Y|X) = 0, then Y is completely determined by X.

acronyms.expansion: Mutual Information between X and Y
acronyms.usage: Use when measuring dependence/relationship between two variables. I(X;Y) = 0 means X and Y are independent.

acronyms.expansion: Kullback-Leibler Divergence from Q to P
acronyms.usage: Use when measuring 'information loss' when using Q to approximate P. Important in Variational Autoencoders, language models.
Use when you need a symmetric version of KL divergence. Always bounded between 0 and 1 (when using log base 2).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bar chart with probability bars. Flat/equal heights = HIGH entropy (maximum uncertainty). Tall narrow spike = LOW entropy (certain outcome). Think: 'Spiky distribution = low entropy, flat distribution = high entropy'

Draw two overlapping circles (Venn diagram). The overlap area = Mutual Information. Circle X = H(X), Circle Y = H(Y), Overlap = I(X;Y). Non-overlapping parts = remaining uncertainties H(X|Y) and H(Y|X).

Think of two probability distribution curves (like bell curves). KL divergence measures 'how much area/height differs' when using Q to approximate P. Visualize as 'one distribution trying to fit another' - the gap is the divergence.
Imagine a 2D grid where rows = X values, columns = Y values. Joint entropy H(X,Y) = total cells filled. Conditional entropy H(Y|X) = uncertainty in column given you know the row.

analogies.analogy: Think of a deck of cards. A perfectly shuffled deck (all cards equally random) = HIGH entropy. A deck sorted by suit and number = LOW entropy. Entropy measures 'how surprised you are' when drawing a card.

Two friends reading the same book and discussing it. Mutual Information is how much new information each gains from the other's perspective. If they read different books (independent), no mutual information. If they have identical notes (dependent), maximum mutual information.

Comparing two recipes for the same dish. KL divergence measures 'how much you'd need to change recipe Q to make it taste like recipe P'. The more different the recipes, the higher the KL divergence. Note: changing P to match Q is different than Q to match P (asymmetric!).

After your friend tells you the weather (X), how uncertain are you about what to wear (Y)? If knowing weather completely determines your outfit, H(Y|X) = 0. If weather doesn't help at all, H(Y|X) = H(Y).
